narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 32, middle family income bracket, full-time enrollment plans, and your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3—and predicted a 67.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 1 school decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your Year 3 school decile ranking. Most influential was your Year 1 decile of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's decile of around 5.7; this early academic struggle strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40—higher than the average of 3.21—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing solid college-level performance. Your Year 3 decile of 2, however, still below the average of about 5.5, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as later-year grades are key indicators of sustained academic stamina needed for law school and the bar. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT of 32 falls notably below the average of 36.7, though it wasn't the top factor here, and your middle income and full-time status align with the majority (over 90% full-time). While these early deciles were the heaviest drags, your stronger undergrad GPA shows real promise that could shift things with improvement. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, and if possible, provide context or updates on those early-year grades through additional coursework or explanations in a new application. Many students in similar situations succeed by targeting schools with holistic reviews or strong support programs for first-generation or Asian American applicants like yourself. Reapplying next cycle with targeted improvements could flip this prediction in your favor.
